bio: John Little McClellan was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Arkansas (1943–1977) after serving in the U.S. House (1935–1943). A segregationist, he chaired the Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations and later the Senate Government Operations Committee. He was influential in labor and internal security matters and opposed much civil rights legislation. He died in office in 1977.

almaMater: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
bio: Samuel J. Ervin Jr. (1896–1985) was a Democratic U.S. senator from North Carolina (1954–1974). A lawyer and former North Carolina judge and legislator, he became a nationally known constitutional expert. Though initially a defender of segregation, he gained prominence for civil-liberties positions and for key roles in investigating Senator Joseph McCarthy and, most famously, chairing the Senate Watergate hearings that helped lead to President Nixon’s resignation.

Roman Lee Hruska was a Nebraska attorney and Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Nebraska (1954–1976). He previously served as Douglas County attorney and as a U.S. Representative from Nebraska (1953–1954). In the Senate he was a prominent conservative voice, served in leadership roles including Senate Republican Whip, and was influential on judicial and law-enforcement issues.

Claiborne Pell (1918–2009) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Rhode Island, serving six terms from 1961 to 1997, the state’s longest-serving senator. He was a leading advocate for education and sponsored legislation that shaped the Basic Educational Opportunity Grant, later renamed the Pell Grant. He also worked on foreign relations and defense issues and authored books after leaving office.

Hubert H. Humphrey (1911–1978) was a Minnesota Democrat who served as U.S. Senator (1949–1964; 1971–1978), mayor of Minneapolis (1945–1948), and U.S. Vice President under Lyndon B. Johnson (1965–1969). A leading liberal voice, he championed civil rights, social welfare, and arms control. He was the 1968 Democratic presidential nominee, losing to Richard Nixon.
Information unavailable.

Robert W. "Bob" Packwood (born 1932) is a retired lawyer and Republican politician from Oregon. He served in the Oregon House of Representatives (1963–1969) before representing Oregon in the U.S. Senate (1969–1995). In the Senate he was influential on tax and trade issues, chaired the Senate Finance Committee, and was active on environmental legislation. He resigned in 1995 amid sexual misconduct allegations.

Roscoe G. Bartlett Jr. (born 1926) was a Republican U.S. Representative from Maryland’s 6th district, serving from 1993 to 2013. A former scientist and engineer, he became known for work on energy policy and fiscal issues and later joined the House Tea Party Caucus. He was among the oldest members of the House at the end of his tenure.

William E. Brock III (1930–2021) was a Republican politician from Tennessee. He served in the U.S. House (1963–1971) and the U.S. Senate (1971–1977). After leaving Congress, he chaired the Republican National Committee (1977–1981) and served in the Reagan administration as U.S. Trade Representative (1981–1985) and Secretary of Labor (1985–1987).

John Ream Curtis (born 1960) is a Utah Republican politician. He served as mayor of Provo (2010–2017) and then won a 2017 special election to represent Utah’s 3rd congressional district, serving in the U.S. House from 2017 to 2025. In 2024, he was elected to the U.S. Senate to succeed Mitt Romney and began serving as Utah’s junior senator in 2025.

almaMater: Yale University; University of Colorado Boulder
bio: Peter H. Dominick was a Colorado attorney and Republican politician who served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1963–1967) and the U.S. Senate (1967–1975). A Yale graduate and University of Colorado Law alumnus, he focused on issues including government reform and foreign affairs. After leaving the Senate, he worked in banking and business and remained active in public and civic affairs.

Paul J. Fannin was an Arizona businessman and Republican politician. He served as Governor of Arizona (1959–1965) and then as U.S. Senator from Arizona (1965–1977). In the Senate he supported Western development and defense-related issues. He retired in 1977 and remained active in business and civic affairs until his death in 2002.

Hiram Leong Fong (1906–2004) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Hawaii (1959–1977) and the first Asian American elected to the U.S. Senate. Previously, he served in the Hawaii Territorial House (1938–1954) and Senate (1954–1959), including as Senate president. In the U.S. Senate he was known as a conservative on fiscal issues and was a candidate for president in 1964.

Barry M. Goldwater (1909–1998) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Arizona (1953–1965; 1969–1987) and the GOP presidential nominee in 1964. A leading figure in modern American conservatism, he opposed much of the New Deal legacy and helped reshape the Republican Party’s conservative movement. He previously served on the Phoenix City Council and later was a major general in the Air Force Reserve.

Clifford P. Hansen was a Republican U.S. Senator from Wyoming (1967–1979) and previously served as Governor of Wyoming (1963–1967). A University of Wyoming–trained attorney, he worked in ranching and law before entering state politics, including service in the Wyoming House. In the Senate he focused on western natural resources and public lands issues. He left the Senate in 1979.

almaMater: Clemson Agricultural College; University of South Carolina
bio: James Strom Thurmond Sr. was a longtime U.S. Senator from South Carolina (1954–2003) and previously served as governor (1947–1951). Elected to the Senate in 1954, he switched from the Democratic to the Republican Party in 1964. He ran for president in 1948 as the States’ Rights (Dixiecrat) candidate and was noted for a record-length filibuster against the Civil Rights Act of 1957.

John Goodwin Tower (1925–1991) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Texas, serving from 1961 to 1985 and becoming the first Texas Republican elected to the Senate since Reconstruction. A World War II Navy veteran, he later worked in broadcasting and academia before entering politics. After leaving the Senate, he chaired the Tower Commission investigating the Iran-Contra affair.

George Stanley McGovern (1922–2012) was a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from South Dakota (1963–1981) after terms in the U.S. House (1957–1961). A WWII bomber pilot and historian, he directed the Food for Peace program under President Kennedy. McGovern was the Democratic presidential nominee in 1972, running on an anti–Vietnam War platform and losing to Richard Nixon.

Quentin N. Burdick was a North Dakota Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1959–1960) and the U.S. Senate (1960–1992). Elected after the death of Senator William Langer, he won multiple reelections and became known for liberal positions and strong support for agriculture, energy, and Native American issues. He chaired the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee and died in office in 1992.

Jennings Randolph was a West Virginia Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1933–1947) and later the U.S. Senate (1958–1985). A long-tenured legislator, he worked on transportation and education issues and was known for supporting expanded voting rights, including efforts toward a constitutional amendment lowering the voting age. He retired in 1985 and was succeeded by Jay Rockefeller.

Clifford P. Case was a Republican U.S. Senator from New Jersey (1955–1979), after serving in the U.S. House (1945–1953). A prominent moderate, he supported civil rights, environmental protection, and government reform, and often broke with his party on social issues. He lost renomination in 1978 to Jeffrey Bell and died in 1982.

Alan Cranston (1914–2000) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from California (1969–1993). A former journalist and California State Controller, he became a leading Senate liberal and served as Senate Democratic Whip (1977–1991). He ran for the 1984 Democratic presidential nomination advocating a nuclear freeze. In 1991 he was reprimanded for his role in the Keating Five savings-and-loan scandal.

almaMater: Georgetown University; University of Michigan Law School
bio: Philip A. Hart was a Michigan Democrat who served as U.S. senator from 1959 until his death in 1976. A World War II Army veteran and former Michigan lieutenant governor, he became known as the "Conscience of the Senate" for his advocacy on civil rights, consumer protection, and immigration reform. The Hart Senate Office Building is named in his honor.

almaMater: Harvard University; University of Virginia School of Law
bio: Edward M. "Ted" Kennedy (1932–2009) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (1962–2009). A leading liberal voice, he chaired major committees and helped shape legislation on health care, education, immigration, civil rights, and labor, including the Americans with Disabilities Act and the Children’s Health Insurance Program. He sought the Democratic presidential nomination in 1980 and was a central figure in the Kennedy political family.

Joseph Manuel Montoya was a New Mexico Democrat who served as lieutenant governor (1947–1951; 1955–1957), then as a U.S. Representative (1957–1964). Appointed to the U.S. Senate in 1964 and later elected, he served as U.S. Senator from New Mexico from 1964 to 1977. He later lost renomination and left the Senate in 1977.

Frank E. "Ted" Moss was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Utah (1959–1977). A lawyer and former Salt Lake City commissioner, he chaired the Senate committee overseeing consumer and environmental health issues and advanced major consumer-protection legislation, including auto safety reforms. He lost reelection in 1976. Moss later worked in government relations and remained active in public affairs until his death in 2003.

Edmund S. Muskie (1914–1996) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Maine (1959–1980), previously serving as governor of Maine (1955–1959) and in the Maine House. A WWII Naval Reserve veteran and lawyer, he was the Democratic vice presidential nominee in 1968. He later served as U.S. Secretary of State under President Jimmy Carter (1980–1981).

Charles H. Percy (1919–2011) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Illinois (1967–1985) and previously an executive at Bell & Howell. In the Senate he was known as a moderate, served as chairman of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee (1977–1979), and worked on foreign policy and domestic issues. After losing reelection in 1984, he remained active in public policy and civic affairs.

Richard S. Schweiker (1926–2015) was a Republican U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania (1961–1969) and U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1969–1981). In the Senate he served on major committees and was known as a comparatively moderate Republican. He was Ronald Reagan’s 1976 primary running mate and later served as U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services (1981–1983) under President Reagan.

almaMater: Middlebury College; University of Michigan Law School
bio: Robert T. Stafford was a Vermont Republican who served in the U.S. House (1959–1961), as Vermont’s governor (1959–1961), and in the U.S. Senate (1971–1989). A liberal “Rockefeller Republican,” he was known for environmental protection and education policy, including major federal student aid legislation. After leaving the Senate, he remained influential in Vermont public life.

almaMater: Yale University; University of California, Berkeley; Harvard Law School
bio: John V. Tunney was a Democratic U.S. Senator from California (1971–1977). Son of boxer Gene Tunney, he previously served in the U.S. House (1965–1971). In the Senate he worked on consumer protection and criminal justice issues. After losing reelection to S. I. Hayakawa, he pursued law and business and later taught and wrote on public policy.

James B. Pearson was a Republican U.S. Senator from Kansas (1962–1967), appointed to fill the vacancy after Andrew F. Schoeppel’s death and later elected to complete the term. A lawyer, he previously served as a Kansas district judge and was active in state Republican politics. He did not win reelection in 1966, losing the GOP primary to Bob Dole, and returned to legal and civic work.

Robert Foster Bennett (1933–2016) was a Republican U.S. senator from Utah (1993–2011). A businessman and political operative, he previously served as president and CEO of the Franklin Institute. In the Senate he worked on banking, appropriations, rules, energy, and economic policy. A generally conservative lawmaker, he lost renomination in 2010 amid Tea Party opposition.

Robert J. "Bob" Dole (1923–2021) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Kansas (1969–1996) and previously a U.S. Representative (1961–1969). A decorated World War II Army veteran, he became a leading Senate Republican, serving as Minority and Majority Leader and chairing key committees. He was the 1976 Republican vice-presidential nominee and the 1996 Republican presidential nominee.

Mark Odom Hatfield (1922–2011) was a Republican U.S. senator from Oregon (1967–1997) and previously served in the U.S. Navy, the Oregon House, and as Oregon secretary of state and governor. A prominent moderate, he was an early opponent of the Vietnam War and a leading appropriator, chairing the Senate Appropriations Committee and advocating arms control and domestic spending priorities.

almaMater: New York University School of Law
bio: Jacob K. Javits was a New York Republican who served in the U.S. House (1947–1954) and U.S. Senate (1957–1981), and as New York Attorney General (1955–1957). A leading liberal Republican, he backed labor, civil rights, and many Great Society initiatives, and became an early critic of the Vietnam War. He helped draft the 1973 War Powers Resolution.

Mike Mansfield (1903–2001) was a Montana Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1943–1953) and U.S. Senate (1953–1977). He became Senate Majority Leader in 1961, leading Democrats for 16 years and guiding major Great Society legislation. After leaving the Senate, he served as U.S. Ambassador to Japan (1977–1988).

Henry M. "Scoop" Jackson was a U.S. Senator from Washington (1953–1983), after serving in the U.S. House (1941–1953). A Cold War Democrat, he was influential on defense and foreign policy and chaired major committees, including Interior and Insular Affairs and Governmental Affairs. He ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 1972 and 1976. He died in office in 1983.

James G. Abourezk (1931–2023) was a South Dakota Democratic politician and attorney. He served one term in the U.S. House (1971–1973) and one term in the U.S. Senate (1973–1979), becoming the first Arab American U.S. senator. After leaving Congress he founded the American-Arab Anti-Discrimination Committee (1980) and was active on civil rights and Middle East policy issues.

Frank Church was a U.S. Senator from Idaho (1957–1981). A Democrat, he became a leading voice on foreign policy, opposing the Vietnam War and sponsoring major environmental legislation including the Wilderness Act. He chaired the Senate Select Committee to Study Governmental Operations with Respect to Intelligence Activities (“Church Committee”), which investigated U.S. intelligence agencies in the 1970s. He lost reelection in 1980.

Pete Domenici was a Republican U.S. Senator from New Mexico (1973–2009), the state’s longest-serving senator. He previously served on the Albuquerque City Commission and practiced law. In the Senate he chaired the Budget Committee and the Energy and Natural Resources Committee, becoming a leading figure on federal budgeting, energy policy, and western water issues. He retired in 2009 and was succeeded by Tom Udall.
Unknown

James A. McClure was a Republican U.S. Senator from Idaho (1973–1993) and previously served in the U.S. House (1967–1973). A lawyer, he became a leading conservative voice on energy and natural-resources policy and chaired the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee. After leaving Congress, he worked in government relations and policy advocacy.

Howard Morton Metzenbaum was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Ohio, first appointed in 1974 and then elected in 1976, serving until 1995. He previously served in the Ohio House and Senate (1943–1951) and was a businessman. In the Senate he was known as a liberal, pro-consumer advocate and served on major committees including Judiciary.

almaMater: San Jose State University; University of Wisconsin–Madison
bio: Gaylord Nelson was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Wisconsin (1963–1981) and previously served as Wisconsin’s governor (1959–1963). In the Senate he was a leading environmental advocate and is best known as the founder of Earth Day (first held in 1970). After leaving office, he worked with the Wilderness Society and remained active in conservation issues.

Thomas F. Eagleton was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Missouri (1968–1987). Previously he served as Missouri attorney general (1961–1965) and lieutenant governor (1965–1969). In 1972 he was the Democratic nominee for vice president with George McGovern but withdrew from the ticket. In the Senate he was known for liberal positions, including opposition to the Vietnam War.

position: U.S. Representative from North Carolina's 7th district
almaMater: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
bio: Charles R. "Charlie" McIntyre (born 1942) is an American politician and attorney. A conservative Democrat, he served in the U.S. House of Representatives for North Carolina's 7th district from 1997 to 2015, after earlier service in the North Carolina Senate (1973–1978). In Congress he was associated with the Blue Dog Coalition and represented the Wilmington area.

almaMater: Howard University; Boston University School of Law
bio: Edward W. Brooke III was a Massachusetts Republican who served as U.S. senator from 1967 to 1979. A World War II Army veteran and lawyer, he was Massachusetts attorney general (1963–1967). Brooke was the first African American elected to the U.S. Senate by popular vote and the first to be re-elected. He later served on corporate and public commissions after leaving the Senate.

Thomas Bryant Cotton is a Republican U.S. senator from Arkansas, serving since 2015. A former U.S. Army infantry officer and Iraq and Afghanistan veteran, he previously represented Arkansas’s 4th congressional district in the U.S. House (2013–2015). Elected to the Senate in 2014, he has been a leading hawkish voice on national security and foreign policy and has chaired major Senate Republican and intelligence roles.

William D. Hathaway was a Maine lawyer and Democrat who served in the U.S. House representing Maine’s 2nd district (1965–1973) and then as U.S. Senator from Maine (1973–1979). He later served as a commissioner of the Federal Maritime Commission (1990–1999).

Harold E. Hughes was a U.S. Senator from Iowa (1969–1975) and a Democrat. A former truck driver and recovering alcoholic, he served as Iowa’s governor (1963–1969) and gained national attention for anti-poverty and civil rights advocacy. In the Senate he focused on labor, health, and social welfare issues, then later served in federal alcoholism and substance-abuse policy roles.

Walter F. "Fritz" Mondale was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Minnesota (1964–1976), appointed after Hubert Humphrey became vice president and later elected. Previously he served as Minnesota attorney general (1960–1964). Mondale was vice president of the United States under Jimmy Carter (1977–1981) and the Democratic presidential nominee in 1984.

almaMater: Northeastern University; Boston University School of Law
bio: John O. Pastore (1907–2000) was a Rhode Island Democrat who served as governor (1945–1950) and U.S. senator (1950–1976). Trained as a lawyer, he was the first Italian American elected to the U.S. Senate. In the Senate he chaired major committees, including Appropriations, and played a prominent role in federal science, health, and education policy oversight.

Edward William Proxmire (1915–2005) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Wisconsin, serving from 1957 to 1989. A noted fiscal watchdog, he created the satirical Golden Fleece Award to highlight what he viewed as wasteful federal spending. He served on the Senate Appropriations Committee, Senate Banking Committee, and the Joint Economic Committee, where he scrutinized military and other government programs.

almaMater: University of Connecticut, University of Chicago
bio: Abraham A. Ribicoff (1910–1998) was a Connecticut Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1949–1961), as governor of Connecticut (1955–1961), U.S. Secretary of Health, Education, and Welfare (1961–1962), and U.S. Senator from Connecticut (1963–1981). He gained national prominence on domestic policy and urban issues, and later worked in private law and consulting.

almaMater: Yale University; University of Virginia School of Law
bio: Lowell P. Weicker Jr. served as a U.S. Representative from Connecticut (1969–1971) and U.S. Senator from Connecticut (1971–1989) as a Republican. A liberal “Rockefeller Republican,” he criticized President Nixon during Watergate and ran for the 1980 Republican presidential nomination. He lost reelection in 1988 to Joe Lieberman. Weicker later left the GOP and was elected governor of Connecticut (1991–1995) on A Connecticut Party ticket.

Harrison A. "Pete" Williams Jr. (1919–2003) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from New Jersey (1959–1982). He previously served in the U.S. House (1953–1957) and in the New Jersey Senate. In the Senate he chaired the Labor and Human Resources Committee and was a leading liberal voice on labor and education. He resigned after being convicted in the FBI ABSCAM corruption investigation.

Rupert Vance Hartke (1919–2003) was a Democratic politician from Indiana. He served as mayor of Evansville before winning election to the U.S. Senate, serving from 1959 to 1977. In the Senate he supported Great Society programs and became a prominent critic of the Vietnam War. He briefly sought the 1972 Democratic presidential nomination and lost reelection in 1976 to Richard Lugar.

almaMater: Montana State University, University of Montana
bio: Lee W. Metcalf was a Montana Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1953–1961) and U.S. Senate (1961–1978). Born in Stevensville, Montana, he was a lawyer and former state judge. In the Senate he became known for environmental and public-lands advocacy and served as the Senate’s permanent acting president pro tempore (1963–1978) until his death in office.

almaMater: Northern Arizona University; University of Arizona
bio: Howard W. Cannon was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Nevada (1959–1983). Born in St. George, Utah, he earned degrees from Northern Arizona University and a J.D. from the University of Arizona. He served in the U.S. Army and Army Air Corps during World War II, later reaching major general in the Air Force Reserve. Cannon also served as city attorney for Las Vegas before the Senate.

Robert A. Taft (1889–1953) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Ohio (1939–1953) and a leading figure in the conservative coalition. Known as “Mr. Republican,” he opposed much of the New Deal’s expansion and emphasized limited government. He co-sponsored the Taft–Hartley Act of 1947. Son of President William Howard Taft, he was a Cincinnati lawyer and briefly served as Senate majority leader in 1953.

almaMater: Indiana University Bloomington; University of Virginia
bio: Birch Evans “Evan” Bayh III (born 1955) is an Indiana Democrat. He served as Indiana secretary of state (1987–1989), then as the state’s 46th governor (1989–1997). Bayh was elected to the U.S. Senate from Indiana in 1998 and served from 1999 to 2011, leaving after announcing he would not seek reelection in 2010.

almaMater: University of Tennessee; University of Cincinnati College of Law
bio: Howard Henry Baker Jr. was a Republican U.S. Senator from Tennessee (1967–1985), serving as Senate Minority Leader (1981–1985) and Majority Leader (1980–1981). A prominent figure during the Watergate era, he was known for a pragmatic, bipartisan style. After leaving the Senate he served as White House Chief of Staff under President Ronald Reagan (1987–1988) and later as U.S. Ambassador to Japan (2001–2005).

Alan Bible was a Nevada Democrat who served as a U.S. Senator from 1954 to 1974, after serving as a U.S. Representative (1949–1954). A World War II Navy veteran, he became a key appropriations and public works legislator and supported major federal investments in Nevada, including water and infrastructure projects. He chaired the Senate Committee on Public Works (later Environment and Public Works).

Lawton Chiles (1930–1998) was a Florida Democrat who served in the U.S. Senate from 1971 to 1989. A Korean War veteran and University of Florida law graduate, he previously served in the Florida House and Florida Senate. Known as “Walkin’ Lawton” for his 1970 campaign walk across Florida, he later became the 41st governor of Florida (1991–1998), serving until his death.

John Culver "Chet" Culver Clark was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Iowa (1973–1979), elected in 1972 after serving as U.S. Representative from Iowa (1965–1973). A former U.S. Navy officer, he later worked in education and public policy. He was defeated for reelection to the Senate in 1978.

Marlow W. Cook was a Republican U.S. Senator from Kentucky (1968–1974). He previously served in the U.S. House representing Kentucky (1957–1963) and as county judge/executive in Louisville–Jefferson County. In the Senate he was known as a moderate-to-liberal Republican and supported civil rights. He later switched to the Democratic Party after leaving office.

Maurice Robert “Mike” Gravel (1930–2021) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Alaska (1969–1981). Previously he served in the Alaska House of Representatives and was Speaker. In the Senate he gained national attention for efforts to end the Vietnam-era draft and for entering the Pentagon Papers into the congressional record in 1971. He later ran for president in 2008 and 2020.

almaMater: Georgetown University; Georgetown University Law Center
bio: Hugh Scott (1900–1994) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1959–1977) and earlier served in the U.S. House from Pennsylvania (1941–1945, 1947–1959). A former lawyer and Army veteran, he became Senate Minority Whip (1969–1973) and Senate Minority Leader (1973–1977). Scott was a prominent GOP figure during the Nixon and Ford years.

Adlai E. Stevenson III was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Illinois (1970–1981). A member of the prominent Stevenson political family, he earlier served as Illinois state treasurer (1965–1967). In the Senate he focused on government oversight and foreign policy issues. After leaving office, he practiced law and remained active in Illinois and national public affairs.

almaMater: Haverford College; University of Maryland School of Law
bio: Charles McCurdy "Mac" Mathias Jr. (1922–2010) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Maryland (1969–1987), previously serving as a U.S. Representative (1961–1969). A moderate-to-liberal Republican, he was noted for support of civil rights, environmental protection, and arms control. He served in the U.S. Navy during World War II and practiced law before entering politics.

Warren G. Magnuson was a Democratic politician from Washington. He served in the U.S. House (1937–1944) and then the U.S. Senate (1944–1981), becoming Washington’s longest-serving senator. A major committee chairman, he shaped consumer protection, health, and commerce policy and served as Senate president pro tempore near the end of his tenure. He was defeated for reelection in 1980.

almaMater: Washington College (now Washington and Lee University)
bio: John Warfield Johnston was a Virginia lawyer and Democratic politician. A former Confederate, he served in the Virginia State Senate and was later elected to the U.S. Senate after Virginia’s readmission following the Civil War, serving 1870–1883. Initially barred under the Fourteenth Amendment, he was eventually seated and became one of the first former Confederates to serve in the U.S. Senate.

Ted Stevens was a Republican U.S. Senator from Alaska (1968–2009) and longtime Senate appropriator. A World War II Army Air Forces veteran, he served as Alaska’s solicitor and later as a federal prosecutor before appointment to the Senate. Stevens became Senate President pro tempore (2007–2009) and chaired the Appropriations Committee, wielding major influence over federal spending for Alaska.

Edward J. Gurney Jr. (1914–1996) was a Republican attorney and U.S. Army officer. He served in the U.S. House representing Florida from 1963 to 1969, then as U.S. Senator from Florida from 1969 to 1974, the first Florida Republican elected to the Senate since Reconstruction. Indicted in an influence-peddling scandal, he resigned in 1974 and was later acquitted.

almaMater: The Citadel; University of South Carolina School of Law
bio: Ernest Frederick “Fritz” Hollings (1922–2019) was a South Carolina Democrat. He served in the South Carolina House (1949–1954), as lieutenant governor (1955–1959), and as governor (1959–1963). Hollings was elected to the U.S. Senate in 1966 and served from 1966 to 2005, becoming one of South Carolina’s longest-serving senators and working for decades alongside Strom Thurmond.

John Glenn Beall Jr. (1927–2006) was a Maryland Republican politician and businessman. He served in the Maryland House of Delegates (1962–1968), the U.S. House of Representatives (1969–1971), and the U.S. Senate (1971–1977). He was the son of Senator J. Glenn Beall and later worked in business after leaving Congress.

Russell B. Long was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Louisiana (1948–1987), succeeding his father, Huey Long. A powerful conservative Democrat, he chaired the Senate Finance Committee (1966–1981), shaping major tax, trade, Social Security, and welfare legislation. Earlier he served as a Navy officer in World War II and worked as a lawyer. After retiring, he remained influential in Louisiana politics.

William V. Roth Jr. (1921–2003) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Delaware (1971–2001). A World War II veteran, he also served in the U.S. House (1967–1971). Roth became best known for sponsoring the Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981, creating the Roth IRA concept. After leaving the Senate, he served as U.S. Ambassador to the Netherlands (2001–2003).

Gale W. McGee was a Wyoming Democrat who served as a U.S. Senator (1959–1977). A former history professor and diplomat, he became a leading advocate for public power, space and science policy, and government reform. After leaving the Senate, he served as U.S. Ambassador to the Organization of American States (1977–1981).

Robert C. Byrd (1917–2010) was a Democratic U.S. senator from West Virginia (1959–2010) and previously a U.S. representative (1953–1959). He served over 51 years in the Senate, the longest tenure of any U.S. senator. Byrd held major leadership roles including Senate Majority Leader, Senate Minority Leader, and President pro tempore, and was a powerful appropriations advocate for West Virginia.

Walter Dee "Dee" Huddleston (1926–2018) was a Kentucky broadcaster and Democratic politician. He served in the Kentucky Senate (1966–1972) before being elected U.S. Senator from Kentucky, serving two terms (1973–1985). He lost his 1984 re-election bid to Mitch McConnell by a narrow margin.

almaMater: Birmingham–Southern College; University of Virginia School of Law
bio: James “Jim” Allen was a conservative Democratic U.S. Senator from Alabama (1969–1979). Previously he served as Alabama’s lieutenant governor (1963–1967) and as a judge on the Alabama circuit court. In the Senate he was known for states’ rights positions and opposition to many liberal initiatives. He died in office in 1979.

Herman Eugene Talmadge (1913–2002) was a Georgia Democrat who served as governor (1947; 1948–1955) and U.S. senator (1957–1981). Initially a prominent segregationist and opponent of civil rights, he later moderated some positions as Southern politics changed. In the Senate he became a leading voice on agriculture and chaired the Senate Agriculture Committee. He lost reelection in 1980 amid ethics and financial controversies.

almaMater: University of Mississippi; Vanderbilt University; University of Alabama
bio: James Oliver Eastland was a Mississippi attorney, plantation owner, and Democratic U.S. senator (1941; 1943–1978). A leading segregationist, he was a prominent opponent of civil rights legislation and a key voice for Southern resistance to racial integration. Eastland chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee for years and wielded substantial influence over judicial nominations and civil rights policy before resigning in 1978.

almaMater: Virginia Polytechnic Institute and State University (Virginia Tech), University of Richmond School of Law
state: Virginia who served in the U
bio: Robert Cortez "Bobby" Scott is a Republican politician from Virginia who served in the U.S. House of Representatives for Virginia's 4th district (1973–1975). A lawyer and businessman, he later served in the Virginia Senate (1978–1995). He won election to Congress in 1972 and was succeeded after one term.

John J. Sparkman was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Alabama (1946–1979) and previously a U.S. Representative (1937–1946). He was a long-serving Senate leader and chaired the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. Sparkman was the Democratic nominee for vice president in 1952, running with Adlai Stevenson. He retired in 1979.

Henry Louis Bellmon was an Oklahoma Republican politician. After serving in the Oklahoma House of Representatives, he became the first Republican governor of Oklahoma (1963–1967). He later served as a U.S. Senator from Oklahoma (1969–1981). Bellmon returned as governor (1987–1991), emphasizing education reform. A World War II veteran, he later struggled with Parkinson’s disease and died in 2009.

almaMater: University of Arkansas; George Washington University; Pembroke College, Oxford

A former state legislator, he focused on energy, natural resources, and transportation issues, reflecting Wyoming’s priorities. He served on several Senate committees and was part of the Senate Republican leadership as secretary of the conference. He died in office in 2007.

Frank R. Lautenberg (1924–2013) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from New Jersey, serving 1982–2001 and 2003 until his death. A former U.S. Army Signal Corps veteran, he became a top executive at Automatic Data Processing (ADP) before entering politics. In the Senate he focused on transportation safety, environmental protection, and public health, and became New Jersey’s longest-serving senator.

almaMater: University of Georgia; University of Michigan
bio: Phil Gramm (born 1942) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Texas (1985–2002) after serving as a U.S. Representative (1979–1985). Originally elected to the House as a Democrat, he switched parties in 1983. An economist, he taught at Texas A&M before politics. In the Senate he became a leading fiscal conservative and influential voice on banking and financial deregulation.

almaMater: University of Louisville; University of Kentucky
bio: Addison Mitchell "Mitch" McConnell III (born 1942) is a Republican U.S. senator from Kentucky, first elected in 1984 and serving since 1985. He became a central Senate leader, serving as Senate Republican leader from 2007 to 2021 and again from 2021 to 2025, including as majority leader from 2015 to 2021. Earlier he was Jefferson County Judge/Executive in Kentucky.

John Davison "Jay" Rockefeller IV served as U.S. Senator from West Virginia (1985–2015) and previously as Governor of West Virginia (1977–1985) and Secretary of State (1969–1973). A Democrat and member of the Rockefeller family, he was known for moderate-to-liberal positions, including health policy leadership as chair of the Senate Commerce Committee.

Albert Arnold Gore Jr. (born 1948) is a Democratic politician and environmental advocate. He served in the U.S. House representing Tennessee (1977–1985) and as U.S. Senator from Tennessee (1985–1993). He resigned from the Senate after being elected vice president, serving under President Bill Clinton (1993–2001). Gore was the 2000 Democratic presidential nominee and later became prominent on climate change.

almaMater: Loyola University Chicago; Loyola University Chicago School of Law
bio: Paul M. Simon was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Illinois (1985–1997) after serving in the U.S. House (1975–1985). Earlier he served in the Illinois House and Illinois Senate and worked as a journalist and editor. In the Senate he was known for liberal positions, strong interest in foreign policy and human rights, and advocacy on education and hunger issues. He later taught at Southern Illinois University.

almaMater: Yale University, Boston College Law School
bio: John Forbes Kerry (born 1943) is a Democratic politician who served as U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (1985–2013). A Vietnam War veteran and former lieutenant in the U.S. Naval Reserve, he later chaired the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. Kerry was the 2004 Democratic presidential nominee, then served as U.S. Secretary of State (2013–2017) and as U.S. climate envoy (2021–2024).

almaMater: Iowa State University; The Catholic University of America (Columbus School of Law)
bio: Tom Harkin (born 1939) served as a Democratic U.S. Senator from Iowa (1985–2015) after representing Iowa’s 5th congressional district in the U.S. House (1975–1985). A Navy jet pilot from 1962 to 1967, he later worked as a congressional aide before winning election to Congress. He won his Senate seat in 1984 and became a prominent liberal voice on labor, health, and disability rights.

almaMater: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
bio: James Thomas Broyhill was a Republican U.S. Representative from North Carolina (1963–1986) who was appointed to the U.S. Senate in 1986 to fill the seat vacated by John P. East. He served briefly until defeated in the Republican primary by Jim Broyhill (actually by Jim Broyhill?); he lost the 1986 GOP primary to Jim Broyhill? (Correction: he lost to Jim Broyhill? )

Brock Adams (1927–2004) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Washington (1987–1993) and previously served as a U.S. Representative from Washington (1965–1977) and U.S. Secretary of Transportation (1977–1979) under President Jimmy Carter. In the Senate he worked on transportation, labor, and Pacific Northwest issues. He was defeated for reelection in 1992.

Christopher S. “Kit” Bond (1939–2015) was a Missouri Republican who served as governor of Missouri (1973–1977, 1981–1985) and as U.S. Senator from Missouri (1987–2011). Earlier he was Missouri attorney general (1971–1973). In the Senate he focused on transportation, appropriations, and intelligence issues, and became a senior member of the Appropriations Committee.

Kent Conrad (born 1948) served as a Democratic U.S. Senator from North Dakota (1987–2013). He previously represented North Dakota at-large in the U.S. House (1985–1987), was North Dakota Tax Commissioner (1977–1981), and served in the state Senate (1981–1985). In the Senate he was known for budget and fiscal policy work, chairing the Senate Budget Committee.

almaMater: South Dakota State University; University of South Dakota
bio: Tom Daschle served as U.S. Representative for South Dakota’s at-large district (1979–1987) and U.S. Senator (1987–2005). A Democrat, he was Senate Minority Leader (1995–2001, 2003–2005) and Senate Majority Leader (2001–2003). He later worked in policy and health-care advocacy and was nominated to be Secretary of Health and Human Services in 2009 but withdrew.

almaMater: Emory University; University of Alabama School of Law
bio: Wyche Fowler Jr. was a Georgia attorney and diplomat who served in Congress as a Democratic U.S. Representative (1975–1987) and U.S. Senator (1987–1993). He previously worked as a legislative aide to Sen. Herman Talmadge. After leaving the Senate, he served as U.S. Ambassador to Saudi Arabia (1996–2001) under President Bill Clinton.

Bob Graham (1936–2024) was a Florida Democrat who served as governor of Florida (1979–1987) and then as U.S. senator (1987–2005). In the Senate he focused on intelligence, national security, and government oversight, chairing the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence and later co-chairing the congressional inquiry into the September 11 attacks. Earlier, he served in the Florida House and Senate.

almaMater: Mount Saint Agnes College; University of Maryland School of Social Work
bio: Barbara A. Mikulski was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Maryland (1987–2017) and previously a U.S. Representative (1977–1987). A former social worker and community organizer, she began her political career on the Baltimore City Council (1971–1976). Elected to the Senate in 1986, she became the first woman elected as U.S. Senator from Maryland and served as a leading appropriator.

almaMater: Utah State University; George Washington University Law School
bio: Harry Reid (1939–2021) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Nevada (1987–2017), previously serving as Nevada’s lieutenant governor (1971–1975) and as a U.S. Representative (1983–1987). A former boxer and attorney, he became Senate Majority Leader (2007–2015) and earlier served as Senate Minority Whip. He played major roles in Democratic legislative strategy and judicial confirmations.

almaMater: Brevard College; University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
bio: Terry Sanford (1917–1998) was a North Carolina Democratic politician and educator. He served as governor of North Carolina (1961–1965), promoting public education and racial moderation, and later founded and led Duke University’s Institute of Policy Sciences and Public Affairs. He was appointed to the U.S. Senate in 1986 and served from 1986 to 1993, focusing on education, budget, and government reform.

Timothy E. Wirth (born 1939) is a Colorado Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1975–1987) and U.S. Senate (1987–1993). He later was Under Secretary of State for Global Affairs in the Clinton administration. Wirth became president of the United Nations Foundation (1998–2013) and has been active in environmental and global policy issues.

almaMater: University of Southwestern Louisiana; Loyola University New Orleans College of Law
bio: John Berlinger Breaux (born 1944) is a Louisiana Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1972–1987) and U.S. Senate (1987–2005). A Southern Democrat associated with the New Democrat Coalition, he was known as one of the party’s more conservative members. After leaving Congress, he became a lobbyist and co-founded the Breaux-Lott Leadership Group.

almaMater: University of Nebraska–Lincoln; University of Nebraska College of Law
bio: David Kemp Karnes was a Republican U.S. Senator from Nebraska, appointed in 1987 and serving until 1989. He was previously an attorney, businessman, and civic leader in Nebraska. After leaving the Senate he worked in law and merchant banking/consulting, including leadership of The Fairmont Group and work with Kutak Rock, and remained active in charitable and educational organizations.

John McCain was a Republican U.S. senator from Arizona (1987–2018), previously a U.S. representative (1983–1987). A Naval Academy graduate and Vietnam War POW, he became known for national security and reform issues. He ran for president in 2000 and was the Republican nominee in 2008, losing to Barack Obama. He chaired the Senate Armed Services Committee.

almaMater: University of Alabama; University of Alabama School of Law
bio: Richard C. "Dick" Shelby (born 1934) is an Alabama lawyer and politician. He served in the Alabama State Senate (1971–1979), then as U.S. Representative from Alabama (1979–1987). Elected to the U.S. Senate in 1986 as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1994 and served until 2023, becoming Alabama’s longest-serving U.S. senator.

Conrad Ray Burns (1935–2016) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Montana (1989–2007). Before the Senate, he served in the Montana Senate and worked as an agricultural educator and broadcaster. In Congress he served on the Senate Appropriations Committee, chaired its Interior Subcommittee, and led a Communications subcommittee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. After leaving office, he worked as a lobbyist.

James M. Jeffords (1934–2014) was a Vermont lawyer and politician. He served in the Vermont Senate (1967–1969) and as Vermont attorney general (1969–1973). Elected to the U.S. House in 1974, he served from 1975 to 1989. He then served as U.S. senator from Vermont (1989–2007), leaving the Republican Party in 2001 to become an independent who caucused with Democrats.

Chester Trent Lott Sr. (born 1941) is a Mississippi Republican who served in the U.S. House (1973–1989) and U.S. Senate (1989–2007). He rose to party leadership, serving as Senate Majority Leader (1996–2001, 2003) and Senate Minority Leader (1995–1996, 2001–2003). He resigned as majority leader in 2002 after controversy over comments about Strom Thurmond.

almaMater: Wheaton College; Indiana University School of Law – Indianapolis
bio: Daniel Ray Coats (born 1943) is a Republican politician from Indiana. He served in the U.S. Army (1966–1968), then represented Indiana’s 4th district in the U.S. House (1981–1989). Appointed to the U.S. Senate in 1989 after Dan Quayle became vice president, he won the 1990 special election and a full term in 1992, serving until 1999. He later was U.S. Ambassador to Germany and Director of National Intelligence.

Herbert H. Kohl (1935–2023) was a Wisconsin businessman and Democrat who served as U.S. senator from Wisconsin from 1989 to 2013. He previously chaired the Wisconsin Democratic Party and ran his family’s Kohl’s department store business. In 1985 he bought the Milwaukee Bucks to keep the team in Milwaukee. In the Senate he focused on consumer protection, education, and government accountability.

almaMater: University of Nevada, Reno; University of California, Hastings College of the Law
bio: Richard H. Bryan is a Nevada attorney and Democratic politician. He served in the Nevada State Senate, then as Nevada attorney general, and was elected governor (1983–1989). Bryan represented Nevada in the U.S. Senate from 1989 to 2001. After leaving the Senate, he returned to private life and legal work.

almaMater: University of Wisconsin–Madison; University of Virginia
bio: Charles S. "Chuck" Robb is a former U.S. Marine officer and Democratic politician. He served as Governor of Virginia (1982–1986) and as U.S. Senator from Virginia (1989–2001). He is married to Lynda Bird Johnson, daughter of President Lyndon B. Johnson. Robb lost his 2000 Senate reelection bid to Republican George Allen and later co-chaired the Iraq Intelligence Commission.

Joseph Robert "Bob" Kerrey (born 1943) is a Democrat who served as governor of Nebraska (1983–1987) and U.S. senator from Nebraska (1989–2001). A U.S. Navy SEAL in Vietnam, he received the Medal of Honor and later faced controversy over the Thanh Phong raid. He ran for the 1992 Democratic presidential nomination and later served as president of The New School (2001–2010).

Connie Mack III (born 1940) is a Republican politician who served as a U.S. Senator from Florida (1989–2001). First elected in 1988, he became known for conservative fiscal and national security positions. He previously worked in business and served as president of the Export-Import Bank of the United States (1987–1988) under President Ronald Reagan.

Joseph I. Lieberman (1942–2024) was a U.S. Senator from Connecticut (1989–2013). A Democrat, he later ran as an Independent and caucused with Senate Democrats. He served in the Connecticut State Senate, was Connecticut attorney general (1983–1989), and gained national prominence as Al Gore’s Democratic vice-presidential running mate in 2000. He was re-elected to the Senate in 1994, 2000, and 2006.
Information unavailable.

almaMater: University of Minnesota; University of Minnesota Law School
bio: Quentin N. Burdick was a North Dakota lawyer and North Dakota Democratic–NPL politician. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1959–1960) and then in the U.S. Senate (1960–1992), representing North Dakota for over three decades. He chaired the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee and was among the Senate’s longest-serving members at his death in 1992.

Clarence James “Bud” Brown Jr. (1927–2022) was a Republican from Ohio. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives for Ohio’s 7th district from 1965 to 1983. After leaving Congress, he joined the Reagan administration as Deputy Secretary of Commerce and served as Acting Secretary of Commerce. He was also a newspaper publisher.

Larry Craig is a Republican politician from Idaho. He served as U.S. Representative for Idaho’s 1st district (1981–1991) and as U.S. Senator from Idaho (1991–2009). Raised on a ranch in Washington County, he earned a BA in political science from the University of Idaho and previously served in the Idaho Senate (1974–1980).
