Multiple individuals could match this abbreviated name, and there is insufficient information to reliably determine the correct Congress member, office held, education, or biography from the dataset details given.

almaMater: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
bio: Paul D. Wellstone (1944–2002) was a Democratic–Farmer–Labor U.S. Senator from Minnesota (1991–2002). An academic and political science professor at Carleton College, he won election in 1990 and became a prominent progressive and populist voice in the Senate. He was killed in a plane crash near Eveleth, Minnesota, days before the 2002 election.

John F. Seymour Jr. is a California Republican who served as a U.S. senator from 1991 to 1992, appointed to fill the vacancy created when Pete Wilson became governor. He previously served in local and regional government in Orange County, including on the Orange County Board of Supervisors. Seymour ran in the 1992 special election to complete the term but lost to Democrat Dianne Feinstein.

almaMater: University of Chicago; Yale University; Howard University School of Law
bio: Harris Wofford was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1991–1995), elected in a 1991 special election after John Heinz’s death. A lawyer and civil rights activist, he worked with John F. Kennedy and Lyndon B. Johnson on civil rights and anti-poverty efforts. He later served as president of Bryn Mawr College (1970–1978) and as Pennsylvania Secretary of Labor and Industry (1987–1991).

almaMater: Brooklyn College; Golden Gate University School of Law
bio: Barbara Boxer (born 1940) served as a U.S. Representative from California (1983–1993) and U.S. Senator from California (1993–2017). Before Congress she was a Marin County supervisor and worked as a journalist. In the Senate she focused on environmental protection, transportation, public works, and women’s health, and chaired the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee (2015–2017).

almaMater: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; University of Chicago
bio: Carol Moseley-Braun (born 1947) is a Democratic politician and diplomat. She served in the Illinois House (1979–1988) and as Cook County Recorder of Deeds (1988–1992). Elected U.S. Senator from Illinois (1993–1999), she was the first African-American woman in the Senate and the first female U.S. senator from Illinois. Defeated in 1998, she later served as U.S. ambassador to New Zealand and Samoa (1999–2001).

almaMater: Brigham Young University, Harvard Business School
bio: Robert F. Bennett (1933–2016) was a Republican U.S. senator from Utah, serving 1993–2011. A businessman and political aide before entering the Senate, he became a senior member of committees including Appropriations, Banking, Rules, Energy, and the Joint Economic Committee. Long viewed as a reliable conservative, he lost renomination in 2010 amid Tea Party opposition.

almaMater: San Diego State University; University of Idaho
bio: Dirk Kempthorne (born 1951) is a Republican politician from Idaho. He served as mayor of Boise (1986–1993), then as U.S. senator from Idaho (1993–1999). He was elected governor of Idaho, serving from 1999 to 2006. Kempthorne became U.S. secretary of the interior under President George W. Bush (2006–2009) and later led the American Council of Life Insurers.

Lauch Faircloth (1928–2023) was a North Carolina politician who served one term as a Republican U.S. senator (1993–1999). Long active as a Democrat, he worked as a political consultant and held state posts including chairman of the North Carolina Highway Commission (1969–1973) and Secretary of Commerce. He switched to the Republican Party in 1991 and defeated Democratic incumbent Terry Sanford in 1992.

almaMater: Columbia University; Georgetown University Law Center
bio: Judd Gregg served as a U.S. Senator from New Hampshire (1993–2011). He previously served in the U.S. House (1981–1989) and as Governor of New Hampshire (1989–1993). A Republican, he was known for fiscal and budget policy work, including as chair of the Senate Budget Committee. After leaving the Senate, he worked in the private sector and served on public-policy boards.

Ben Nighthorse Campbell served as a U.S. Senator from Colorado (1993–2005). A former Olympic judoka and jewelry businessman, he previously served in the U.S. House representing Colorado (1987–1993). In the Senate he focused on Native American affairs and chaired the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs. He was elected as a Democrat but later switched parties.

Byron L. Dorgan served North Dakota in Congress as a U.S. Representative (1981–1992) and U.S. Senator (1992–2011). A Democrat, he previously worked in state government and served as North Dakota tax commissioner. In the Senate he held leadership roles, chaired the Democratic Policy Committee, and led the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs. He announced in 2010 he would not seek re-election.

almaMater: University of Wisconsin–Madison; Magdalen College, Oxford; Harvard Law School
bio: Russell D. Feingold (born 1953) is a Democratic politician and lawyer who served as U.S. Senator from Wisconsin (1993–2011) after serving in the Wisconsin State Senate (1983–1993). With John McCain, he coauthored the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act of 2002 and received the 1999 Profile in Courage Award. He was the only senator to vote against the 2001 Patriot Act and lost reelection in 2010.

Dianne Feinstein (1933–2023) was a Democratic U.S. senator from California, serving from 1992 until her death. A longtime San Francisco leader, she served on the city’s Board of Supervisors and became mayor in 1978 after the assassinations of George Moscone and Harvey Milk, holding the office until 1988. In the Senate, she became a senior figure and chaired the Intelligence Committee.

Robert C. Krueger was a Texas Democrat who served briefly in the U.S. House (1975) and later as a U.S. Senator from Texas (1993), appointed to fill the vacancy after Lloyd Bentsen joined the Clinton administration. He previously served in the Texas Senate and ran unsuccessfully for the 1994 Democratic Senate nomination. Krueger later served as U.S. Ambassador to Burundi (1994–1996).

Patty Murray is a Democratic U.S. senator from Washington, serving since 1993 and the state’s first female senator. Raised in Bothell, she earned a B.A. in physical education from Washington State University and worked as a preschool and parenting teacher. She entered politics via a local school board, then served in the Washington State Senate (1989–1993). She was Senate president pro tempore from 2023 to 2025.

Paul Douglas Coverdell (1939–2000) was a Republican U.S. senator from Georgia, serving from 1993 until his death. Before the Senate, he was director of the Peace Corps (1989–1991) under President George H. W. Bush. He also served in the Georgia State Senate and worked in business and civic roles. He was known for foreign policy and public service initiatives.

Kay Bailey Hutchison (born 1943) is a Texas Republican who served as a U.S. Senator from Texas (1993–2013), first elected in a 1993 special election and later re-elected three times. Earlier she served in the Texas House (1972–1976) and as Texas State Treasurer (1991–1993). She later served as U.S. Permanent Representative to NATO (2017–2021).

Harlan Mathews (1927–2014) was a Tennessee Democratic politician appointed to the U.S. Senate (1993–1994) to fill Al Gore’s seat after Gore became vice president. A longtime state official, he served in Tennessee government for decades, including in the state legislature and executive posts such as commissioner of finance and administration and commissioner of transportation, before returning to private life.

Spencer Abraham (born 1952) is a Republican attorney and politician who served as U.S. Senator from Michigan (1995–2001). He later served as the 10th U.S. Secretary of Energy (2001–2005) under President George W. Bush. He is a founder of the Federalist Society and co-founded the Harvard Journal of Law & Public Policy.

almaMater: Yale University; University of Chicago Law School
bio: John Ashcroft (born 1942) is a Missouri Republican who served as U.S. Senator from Missouri (1995–2001). He previously held statewide office as Missouri state auditor (1973–1975), attorney general (1976–1985), and governor (1985–1993). Ashcroft later served as U.S. attorney general under President George W. Bush (2001–2005) and afterward worked as a lobbyist and founded The Ashcroft Group.

almaMater: Miami University; Ohio Northern University College of Law
bio: Mike DeWine is an Ohio Republican politician and attorney. He served in the U.S. House (1983–1991) and the U.S. Senate (1995–2007). He was Ohio’s lieutenant governor (1991–1994), Ohio attorney general (2011–2019), and became governor of Ohio in 2019. Born in Yellow Springs, he earned a BA from Miami University and a JD from Ohio Northern University.

Bill Frist (born 1952) is a Republican physician and former U.S. Senator from Tennessee (1995–2007). A cardiothoracic transplant surgeon, he founded the Vanderbilt Transplant Center before entering politics. He defeated incumbent Jim Sasser in 1994, later chaired the National Republican Senatorial Committee, and served as Senate Majority Leader (2003–2007), helping advance parts of President George W. Bush’s domestic agenda.

Rod Grams (1948–2013) was a Minnesota television news anchor turned politician. After working as a senior anchor at KMSP-TV, he was elected to the U.S. House in 1992 from Minnesota’s 6th district. He then won election to the U.S. Senate in 1994, serving one term (1995–2001) before losing reelection to Mark Dayton in 2000.

James M. Inhofe (1934–2024) was a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Oklahoma (1994–2023), the state’s longest-serving senator. He previously served as U.S. representative for Oklahoma’s 1st district (1987–1994) and in Oklahoma state and local offices. In the Senate he became a leading conservative voice on defense and environmental policy, including chairing the Senate Armed Services Committee.

Jon Kyl is a Republican politician and lobbyist who served as U.S. Representative from Arizona (1987–1995) and U.S. Senator from Arizona (1995–2013). He was Senate Minority Whip from 2007 to 2013. After retiring, he worked as a lobbyist. He briefly returned to the Senate in 2018 after John McCain’s death, serving until early 2019.

almaMater: Pennsylvania State University; University of Pittsburgh
bio: Rick Santorum is a Republican politician who served as U.S. Representative from Pennsylvania’s 18th district (1991–1995) and U.S. Senator from Pennsylvania (1995–2007). He rose to Senate Republican leadership as conference chair and was known for strong social conservative positions. He lost reelection in 2006 to Bob Casey Jr. Santorum later ran for the 2012 Republican presidential nomination.

almaMater: University of Alabama; University of Alabama School of Law
bio: Richard C. Shelby (born 1934) served as U.S. senator from Alabama (1987–2023). Elected in 1986 as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1994. Previously he was a Tuscaloosa city prosecutor, a U.S. magistrate, and served in the Alabama State Senate (1971–1978) and the U.S. House (1979–1987).

Olympia J. Snowe is a Maine Republican who served in Congress for decades, including as U.S. Representative from Maine (1979–1995) and U.S. Senator (1995–2013). Known as a centrist, she often influenced close Senate votes and helped broker bipartisan agreements. She announced in 2012 she would not seek reelection, citing increasing partisanship, and left office in 2013.

Craig Lyle Thomas (1933–2007) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Wyoming (1995–2007). He previously served as Wyoming’s at-large U.S. Representative after winning a 1989 special election, and later won reelection. Before Congress, he worked in agriculture and rural development, including leadership with the Wyoming Farm Bureau and service in the Wyoming House of Representatives (1984–1989). He was known for agriculture and rural policy expertise.

Fred Dalton Thompson (1942–2015) was a Republican U.S. Senator from Tennessee (1994–2003), appointed to fill Al Gore’s former seat and later elected. A lawyer and former counsel to the Senate Watergate Committee, he also worked as a lobbyist and became a nationally known actor. He ran unsuccessfully for the 2008 Republican presidential nomination.

Sheila Frahm (born 1945) is a Kansas Republican politician who briefly served in the U.S. Senate in 1996 after being appointed to fill the vacancy created by Sen. Nancy Kassebaum’s resignation. She later ran in the 1996 special election to complete the term but lost the Republican primary to Sam Brownback. She was previously active in Kansas state and local public service.

almaMater: Stanford University; University of California, Santa Barbara
bio: Ron Wyden is a Democratic U.S. senator from Oregon, first elected in a 1996 special election and serving continuously since. He previously represented Oregon in the U.S. House (1981–1996). In the Senate he has been a leading voice on tax and health policy and is known for privacy and civil-liberties advocacy, including opposition to expansive government surveillance. He has chaired major committees, including Senate Finance.

Alan Wayne Allard (born 1943) is a Republican politician and veterinarian from Colorado. He served in the Colorado State Senate (1983–1991), the U.S. House representing Colorado’s 4th district (1991–1997), and the U.S. Senate (1997–2009). He did not seek re-election in 2008 and later worked as a Washington lobbyist.

Chuck Hagel is an American politician and Vietnam War veteran who served as U.S. Senator from Nebraska (1997–2009). A Republican, he was elected in 1996 and reelected in 2002, then chose not to run in 2008. After the Senate, he served as chair of the President’s Intelligence Advisory Board (2009–2013) and as U.S. Secretary of Defense (2013–2015).

almaMater: Bob Jones University; University of Arkansas
bio: Tim Hutchinson (born 1949) is a Republican politician and lobbyist from Arkansas. He served as U.S. Representative for Arkansas's 3rd district (1993–1997) and then as U.S. Senator from Arkansas (1997–2003), the first Arkansas Republican senator since Reconstruction. He lost reelection in 2002 to Mark Pryor. After leaving the Senate, he worked as a lobbyist.

almaMater: George Washington University; University of Denver
bio: Michael B. Enzi was a Republican U.S. Senator from Wyoming (1997–2021). An Air Force/Air National Guard veteran, he previously served as mayor of Gillette, in the Wyoming House of Representatives (from 1987), and in the Wyoming Senate. In Washington he focused on fiscal policy, health and education issues, and served on major Senate committees. He was among Wyoming’s longest-serving senators.

almaMater: Kansas State University; University of Kansas
bio: Pat Roberts is a Republican who served Kansas in Congress. He was a U.S. Representative from Kansas (1981–1997) before serving as U.S. Senator (1997–2023). A former aide to Rep. Keith Sebelius, he became known for agricultural policy and chaired the Senate Agriculture Committee, as well as the Senate Intelligence Committee (2015–2020).

Jeff Sessions (born 1946) is an Alabama Republican. He served as U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of Alabama (1981–1993), then Alabama attorney general (1995–1997) and U.S. senator from Alabama (1997–2017). He resigned from the Senate to become U.S. attorney general (2017–2018) in the Trump administration.

almaMater: Kansas State University; University of Kansas
bio: Samuel Dale Brownback (born 1956) is a Kansas Republican. He served as U.S. Representative for Kansas’s 2nd district (1995–1996) and U.S. Senator from Kansas (1996–2011). After leaving the Senate, he was governor of Kansas (2011–2018) and later served as U.S. Ambassador-at-Large for International Religious Freedom (2018–2021). He ran for the 2008 Republican presidential nomination.

almaMater: Georgetown University (School of Foreign Service); Georgetown University Law Center
bio: Richard J. Durbin is a Democratic politician and attorney from Illinois. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives (1983–1997) before becoming U.S. senator in 1997. He has been Senate Democratic Whip since 2005, serving as the party’s No. 2 leader, and chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee (2021–2025).

Thomas Francis “Tom” Johnson (1894–1969) was a Democratic U.S. Senator from South Dakota (1949–1963). A former University of Minnesota economics professor and WWII-era federal official, he was elected to the Senate in 1948 and served until retiring in 1963. He was succeeded by George McGovern.

almaMater: Rutgers University; Georgetown University Law Center
bio: Robert G. Torricelli (born 1951) is a New Jersey Democrat who served in the U.S. House (1983–1997) and U.S. Senate (1997–2003). He chaired the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee. In 2002 he ended his re-election campaign after an ethics admonishment tied to campaign finance issues. After leaving Congress, he worked in consulting and real estate.

Susan M. Collins (born 1952) is a Republican U.S. senator from Maine, serving since 1997. Raised in Caribou, Maine, she graduated from St. Lawrence University. She began her career as a staff assistant to Senator William Cohen, later serving as staff director for a Senate Governmental Affairs subcommittee. She also served in Maine state government, including as commissioner of the Department of Professional and Financial Regulation.

almaMater: United States Military Academy; Harvard University; Harvard Law School
bio: John F. Reed (born 1949) is a Democratic politician and former U.S. Army officer from Rhode Island. He served as U.S. Representative for Rhode Island’s 2nd district (1991–1997) before being elected to the U.S. Senate in 1996. A West Point and Harvard graduate with a Harvard Law J.D., he has focused heavily on defense and appropriations issues.

Ben Nighthorse Campbell (1933–2025) was a U.S. Air Force veteran and Cheyenne leader who served Colorado in Congress. He represented Colorado’s 3rd district in the U.S. House (1987–1993) and served as U.S. Senator from Colorado (1993–2005). Elected as a Democrat, he switched to the Republican Party in 1995 and was reelected in 1998. He retired in 2005.

Joseph Maxwell Cleland was a Georgia Democrat and decorated Vietnam War veteran who lost three limbs in combat. He served in the Georgia State Senate (1971–1975), was Administrator of Veterans Affairs under President Jimmy Carter (1977–1981), and Georgia Secretary of State (1982–1996). Cleland represented Georgia in the U.S. Senate from 1997 to 2003, then held federal appointments including Export-Import Bank director and American Battle Monuments Commission.

Mary Landrieu (born 1955) is a Louisiana Democrat who served in the Louisiana House (1980–1988) and as state treasurer (1988–1996). She was U.S. senator from Louisiana (1997–2015), known for post–Hurricane Katrina advocacy and for chairing the Senate Small Business and Entrepreneurship Committee (2009–2014) and the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee (2014–2015).

almaMater: Brigham Young University; Southwestern University School of Law
bio: Gordon H. Smith (born 1952) is an Oregon politician and businessman who served as a Republican U.S. Senator from Oregon (1997–2009). Before the Senate he served in the Oregon State Senate and as president and CEO of the Smith Frozen Foods company. After leaving office, he became president of the National Association of Broadcasters (2009–2022) and later held leadership roles in the LDS Church.

Charles E. Schumer (born 1950) is a Democrat serving as U.S. senator from New York since 1999. A Brooklyn native, he previously served in the New York State Assembly (1975–1980) and in the U.S. House of Representatives (1981–1999), representing Brooklyn-based districts. He became Senate Democratic leader in 2017 and served as Senate Majority Leader (2021–2025).

almaMater: Brigham Young University; Harvard Law School
bio: Michael D. Crapo (born 1951) is a Republican U.S. senator from Idaho, serving since 1999. He previously represented Idaho’s 2nd congressional district in the U.S. House (1993–1999). Before Congress he served in the Idaho Senate (1984–1992), including as president pro tempore. A lawyer, he graduated from Brigham Young University and Harvard Law School.

Peter Gosselin Fitzgerald (born 1960) is a retired lawyer and Republican politician. He served in the Illinois State Senate (1993–1998) and then as U.S. senator from Illinois (1999–2005), after defeating incumbent Carol Moseley Braun in 1998. Known as a maverick, he declined to seek reelection and was succeeded by Barack Obama. After leaving politics, he co-founded Chain Bridge Bank.

Blanche Lambert Lincoln (born 1960) is an Arkansas Democrat who served in the U.S. House representing Arkansas’s 1st district (1993–1997) and in the U.S. Senate (1999–2011). Elected to the Senate in 1998, she became the first woman elected U.S. senator from Arkansas since 1932. She chaired the Senate Agriculture Committee and lost her 2010 reelection bid to Republican John Boozman.

almaMater: Indiana University Bloomington; University of Virginia
bio: Birch Evans “Evan” Bayh III (born 1955) is an Indiana Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from Indiana (1999–2011). Previously, he was Indiana secretary of state (1987–1989) and the 46th governor of Indiana (1989–1997). Son of Senator Birch Bayh, he was elected to the Senate in 1998 and reelected in 2004, then announced in 2010 he would not seek reelection.

Jim Bunning (1931–2017) was a Kentucky Republican who served in the U.S. House (1987–1999) and U.S. Senate (1999–2011). Before politics he was a Major League Baseball pitcher (1955–1971), throwing a 1964 perfect game for the Philadelphia Phillies, and was elected to the National Baseball Hall of Fame. In Congress he was known as a fiscal conservative.

Lincoln D. Chafee (born 1953) is a Rhode Island politician who served as U.S. Senator (1999–2007), appointed after his father John Chafee’s death and elected in 2000. He previously was mayor of Warwick (1993–1999). After leaving the Senate, he served as Rhode Island’s governor (2011–2015). He later changed party affiliation from Republican to independent, then Democrat, and became a Libertarian.

almaMater: North Carolina State University; University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
bio: John Edwards (born 1953) is an American lawyer and former U.S. Senator from North Carolina (1999–2005). Elected in 1998, he defeated Republican incumbent Lauch Faircloth. Edwards ran for the Democratic presidential nomination in 2004 and 2008 and was the 2004 Democratic vice-presidential nominee on John Kerry’s ticket. He declined to seek Senate re-election in 2004.

almaMater: Ohio University; Case Western Reserve University
bio: George V. Voinovich (1936–2016) was a Republican politician who served as U.S. senator from Ohio (1999–2011). He previously was mayor of Cleveland (1980–1989) and governor of Ohio (1991–1998). Earlier he served as Ohio state auditor and in other state and local roles. He was known as a pragmatic, business-oriented Republican and spent over four decades in public service.

Zell Bryan Miller (1932–2018) was a Georgia Democrat who served as lieutenant governor (1975–1991), governor (1991–1999), and U.S. senator from Georgia (2000–2005), appointed after Paul Coverdell’s death and later elected. As a senator he was a conservative Democrat and notably endorsed Republican George W. Bush in 2004. He did not seek reelection.

Jean Carnahan was a Missouri Democrat and former first lady of Missouri (1993–2000). After her husband, Gov. Mel Carnahan, died shortly before the 2000 election but won Missouri’s U.S. Senate race posthumously, she was appointed to the seat and served as Missouri’s junior U.S. senator from 2001 to 2002. She later worked as a writer and remained active in public affairs.

Hillary Rodham Clinton (born 1947) is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from New York (2001–2009). Earlier she was First Lady of the United States (1993–2001) and worked as an attorney and policy advocate. After the Senate, she was U.S. Secretary of State (2009–2013) and later the 2016 Democratic presidential nominee.

Jon Corzine is a Democrat who served as U.S. Senator from New Jersey (2001–2006). A former Goldman Sachs executive, he entered politics after a finance career and later became New Jersey’s 54th governor (2006–2010). He lost reelection in 2009 to Chris Christie. After leaving office, he became CEO of MF Global, which collapsed in 2011.

Mark Dayton (born 1947) is a Minnesota Democratic–Farmer–Labor politician. He served as Minnesota state auditor (1991–1995), U.S. senator from Minnesota (2001–2007), and governor of Minnesota (2011–2019). A Yale graduate, he previously worked in teaching and social work and served as a legislative aide to Sen. Walter Mondale and Gov. Rudy Perpich.

Gaylord A. Nelson was a Democratic U.S. Senator from Wisconsin (1963–1981) and previously served as Wisconsin governor (1959–1963). In the Senate he was known for conservation and environmental legislation and is widely credited as the founder of Earth Day (first held in 1970). After leaving office, he worked on environmental issues and later received the Presidential Medal of Freedom.

Deborah “Debbie” Stabenow (born 1950) is a Michigan Democrat who served as a U.S. senator from 2001 to 2025, Michigan’s first female senator. She previously represented Michigan’s 8th district in the U.S. House (1997–2001) and served in Michigan state and local government. In the Senate she chaired the Agriculture Committee (2011–2015, 2021–2025) and led the Senate Democratic Policy Committee.

almaMater: Oregon State University; Colorado State University
bio: John E. Ensign is a Republican former U.S. Senator from Nevada (2001–2011). Previously, he represented Nevada’s 1st congressional district in the U.S. House (1995–1999). A veterinarian by training, he earned degrees from Oregon State University and a D.V.M. from Colorado State University. He resigned from the Senate in 2011 amid a Senate Ethics Committee investigation related to an extramarital affair.

Maria Cantwell (born 1958) is a Washington Democrat serving as a U.S. senator since 2001. She previously served in the Washington House of Representatives (1987–1993) and one term in the U.S. House representing Washington’s 1st district (1993–1995). After working at RealNetworks, she returned to politics and narrowly defeated Republican Sen. Slade Gorton in 2000.

George F. Allen (born 1952) is a Virginia Republican politician and lawyer. He served in the Virginia House of Delegates (1983–1991), then as U.S. Representative for Virginia's 7th district (1991–1993). He was Governor of Virginia (1994–1998) and later U.S. Senator from Virginia (2001–2007), after defeating Sen. Chuck Robb in 2000.

almaMater: University of Nebraska–Lincoln; University of Nebraska College of Law
bio: E. Benjamin "Ben" Nelson (born 1941) is a Nebraska politician, attorney, and former insurance executive. A Democrat, he served as governor of Nebraska (1991–1999), then as U.S. senator from Nebraska (2001–2013). He lost a 1996 Senate race to Chuck Hagel, won election in 2000, and was reelected in 2006. After leaving the Senate, he worked in business and advocacy roles.

Dean M. Barkley (born 1950) is a Minnesota attorney and politician. Founder and chair of the Minnesota Reform Party (later Independence Party), he managed Jesse Ventura’s 1998 gubernatorial campaign and later served in Ventura’s administration. After Sen. Paul Wellstone’s death, Ventura appointed Barkley to the U.S. Senate, where he served from 2002 to 2003 until Republican Norm Coleman took office.

Mark Lunsford Pryor (born 1963) is an Arkansas attorney and Democrat. He served in the Arkansas House of Representatives (1991–1995), was Arkansas Attorney General (1999–2003), and represented Arkansas in the U.S. Senate (2003–2015). The son of former Governor and U.S. Senator David Pryor, he lost reelection in 2014 to Tom Cotton and later worked as a lobbyist.

Lindsey Olin Graham (born 1955) is a Republican U.S. senator from South Carolina, serving since 2003. He previously represented South Carolina's 3rd district in the U.S. House (1995–2003) and served in the South Carolina House (1993–1995). An attorney, he served in the U.S. Air Force JAG Corps and later the Air Force Reserve, reaching colonel. He chaired the Senate Judiciary Committee (2019–2021).

almaMater: University of Georgia; University of Tennessee
bio: Saxby Chambliss (born 1943) is a Republican lawyer and retired politician from Georgia. He served in the U.S. House of Representatives for Georgia’s 8th district (1995–2003), including chairing the House Intelligence Subcommittee on Terrorism and Homeland Security. Elected to the U.S. Senate in 2002, he represented Georgia from 2003 to 2015 and chaired the Senate Agriculture Committee (2005–2007).

Norm Coleman (born 1949) is a Minnesota politician and attorney. Originally elected as a Democratic–Farmer–Labor member, he switched to the Republican Party in 1996. He served as mayor of Saint Paul (1994–2002) and then as U.S. senator from Minnesota (2003–2009). He won the Senate seat in 2002 and lost a closely contested reelection bid in 2008.

almaMater: Vanderbilt University; New York University School of Law
bio: Lamar Alexander (born 1940) is a Tennessee Republican who served as U.S. senator from 2003 to 2021. He previously was governor of Tennessee (1979–1987) and U.S. secretary of education (1991–1993) under President George H. W. Bush. An attorney and academic administrator, he supported education reform initiatives and later held Senate leadership and committee roles.

almaMater: Trinity University; St. Mary's University School of Law; University of Virginia School of Law
bio: John Cornyn III is a Republican U.S. Senator from Texas, first elected in 2002. Born in Houston, he previously served as a Texas district judge (1985–1991), justice of the Texas Supreme Court (1991–1997), and Texas Attorney General (1999–2002). In the Senate he chaired the National Republican Senatorial Committee (2009–2013) and served as Senate Republican whip (2013–2019).

Elizabeth H. Dole is a Republican politician and attorney who served as U.S. Senator from North Carolina (2003–2009). She previously held senior executive-branch roles, including U.S. Secretary of Transportation under Ronald Reagan (1983–1987) and U.S. Secretary of Labor under George H. W. Bush (1989–1990). She was president of the American Red Cross (1991–1999) and sought the 2000 Republican presidential nomination.

almaMater: Massachusetts Institute of Technology; Harvard University
bio: John E. Sununu (born 1964) is a Republican politician from New Hampshire. He served as U.S. Representative for New Hampshire’s 1st district (1997–2003) and then as U.S. Senator from New Hampshire (2003–2009), noted as the Senate’s youngest member throughout his term. He lost reelection in 2008 to Democrat Jeanne Shaheen. He is the son of former New Hampshire governor and White House chief of staff John H. Sununu.

almaMater: Washington University in St. Louis; University of Chicago
bio: James "Jim" Talent (born 1956) is a Republican politician from Missouri. He served in the U.S. House representing Missouri’s 2nd district (1993–2001), then won a 2002 special election for the U.S. Senate, serving from 2002 to 2007. He lost reelection in 2006 to Claire McCaskill. After office he worked as a policy adviser, including with Mitt Romney, and at the Heritage Foundation.

Lisa Murkowski (born 1957) is a Republican U.S. senator from Alaska, first appointed in 2002 to fill the seat vacated by her father, Gov. Frank Murkowski. Previously she served in the Alaska House of Representatives (1999–2002). She won election to a full Senate term in 2004 and has since been reelected, often as a pragmatic, centrist-leaning Republican.

almaMater: Ohio State University; University of Delaware
bio: Tom Carper is a Democratic politician from Delaware. A former U.S. Navy officer, he served as Delaware state treasurer (1977–1983), U.S. representative (1983–1993), and governor of Delaware (1993–2001). He was elected to the U.S. Senate in 2000 and served from 2001 to 2025, focusing on fiscal issues, transportation, and environmental policy.
